bug.summary: In both `prepare_from_checkpoint` and `prepare_from_branch`, when the sandbox is Daytona and devcontainer is enabled, `devcontainer_bridge::devcontainer_to_snapshot_config()` is never called. The Daytona sandbox is created with `resolve_daytona_config(None, run_defaults).unwrap_or_default()` before the devcontainer config is resolved, so the devcontainer Dockerfile is never bridged into the Daytona snapshot config. The resumed run will use the default sandbox image instead of the custom devcontainer image.

extended_reasoning.what_the_bug_is: In `run.rs` (lines 1050-1054), when devcontainer is enabled and the sandbox is Daytona, `devcontainer_bridge::devcontainer_to_snapshot_config(&dc)` converts the devcontainer Dockerfile into a snapshot config that is merged into `daytona_config` before `DaytonaSandbox::new()` is called. In `resume.rs`, this bridge step is completely absent — `devcontainer_bridge` is never imported or called. Both `prepare_from_checkpoint` (line 301) and `prepare_from_branch` (line 566) create the Daytona sandbox with the raw `resolve_daytona_config()` result, which does not include the devcontainer snapshot.
extended_reasoning.the_specific_code_path_that_triggers_it: User has a project with `devcontainer.json` containing a custom Dockerfile, sandbox configured as Daytona, and runs `fabro resume <run-id>` or `fabro resume --checkpoint <path> --workflow <path>`. The devcontainer config is resolved (lines 211-225 or 587-601), but the Daytona sandbox was already created at lines 300-315 or 565-580 without the devcontainer snapshot config.
extended_reasoning.why_existing_code_does_not_prevent_it: The devcontainer resolution happens AFTER the sandbox is created in both prepare functions. In `run.rs`, the devcontainer is resolved at lines 1028-1054 which modifies `daytona_config` BEFORE the sandbox creation at line 1189. The ordering in `resume.rs` is inverted — sandbox creation comes first, devcontainer resolution comes after.
extended_reasoning.impact: Resuming a Daytona + devcontainer workflow will spin up a sandbox with the default Daytona image instead of the custom devcontainer image. The agent will be working in a wrong environment — missing dependencies, wrong base OS, etc. This could cause stages to fail with confusing errors or produce incorrect results.
extended_reasoning.how_to_fix_it: Move the devcontainer resolution (and the `devcontainer_bridge::devcontainer_to_snapshot_config()` call) to happen BEFORE the Daytona sandbox creation in both `prepare_from_checkpoint` and `prepare_from_branch`. The resolved devcontainer config should be used to override the Daytona config's snapshot field before passing it to `DaytonaSandbox::new()`, matching the pattern in `run.rs` lines 1050-1054.

step_by_step_proof.step: User has a project with `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` containing a custom Dockerfile, and `fabro.toml` with `[sandbox] provider = "daytona"` and `devcontainer = true`.
User runs `fabro resume <run-id>`, entering `prepare_from_branch()`.
At line 566, `resolve_daytona_config(None, run_defaults).unwrap_or_default()` creates a Daytona config without any snapshot (devcontainer Dockerfile is not included).
At line 567, `DaytonaSandbox::new(config, ...)` creates the sandbox with the default image.
At lines 587-601, the devcontainer config is resolved, but it is only used for `devcontainer_phases` and `devcontainer_env` — it is never bridged to the Daytona config.
The `devcontainer_bridge::devcontainer_to_snapshot_config()` function (used in `run.rs` at line 1051) is never called in `resume.rs` — it is not even imported.
The same issue affects `prepare_from_checkpoint` at lines 300-315 (sandbox creation) and 211-225 (devcontainer resolution).
The resumed workflow runs in a default Daytona sandbox image instead of the custom devcontainer image, causing environment mismatch.

bug.summary: In resume.rs, after `apply_goal_override` is called in both `prepare_from_checkpoint` (line 179) and `prepare_from_branch` (line 432), `resolve_file_ref()` is never invoked on the goal attribute. In run.rs, lines 581-591 explicitly resolve `@file` references after `apply_goal_override`. This means `fabro resume --goal '@goals/plan.md'` will use the literal string `@goals/plan.md` as the goal text instead of reading the file contents.

extended_reasoning.what_the_bug_is: The `resolve_file_ref()` function, which expands `@path` references into file contents, is called in `run.rs` after `apply_goal_override` but is completely absent from `resume.rs`. Both `prepare_from_checkpoint` and `prepare_from_branch` call `apply_goal_override` but skip the file reference resolution step.
extended_reasoning.the_specific_code_path_that_triggers_it: User runs `fabro resume --goal '@goals/plan.md'` (or any `@file` reference). The `resolve_cli_goal` function returns the literal string `@goals/plan.md`. `apply_goal_override` sets it as the graph's `goal` attribute. The workflow engine then uses the literal `@goals/plan.md` string as the goal, rather than the contents of the file.
extended_reasoning.why_existing_code_does_not_prevent_it: There is no other place in the resume code path that resolves `@file` references. The `prepare_with_file_inlining` call on the workflow source handles `@` references within the Graphviz source, but the goal override happens after that step, so a CLI-provided `--goal '@file'` is never resolved.
extended_reasoning.impact: Any user passing a `--goal` flag with an `@file` reference to `fabro resume` will get incorrect behavior. The agent will receive the literal path string as its goal instead of the file's contents, leading to a nonsensical or failed workflow run.
extended_reasoning.how_to_fix_it: After each `apply_goal_override` call in resume.rs (lines 179 and 432), add the same `resolve_file_ref` block from run.rs lines 581-591: check if the graph has a `goal` attribute, call `fabro_workflows::transform::resolve_file_ref()` on it, and update the attribute if the resolved value differs.

step_by_step_proof.step: User runs `fabro resume --checkpoint path/to/checkpoint --goal '@goals/plan.md'`
`resolve_cli_goal` returns `Some("@goals/plan.md")`
`apply_goal_override` sets the graph's `goal` attribute to the literal string `@goals/plan.md`
In run.rs, lines 581-591 would call `resolve_file_ref` to read `goals/plan.md` and replace the attribute with the file contents — but this code is absent from resume.rs
The workflow engine receives `@goals/plan.md` as the literal goal text
Bug: the agent works on the wrong goal

bug.summary: `progress_ui.finish()` is called at line 1025 of resume.rs before `generate_retro()` runs at line 1127. In run.rs, the comment explicitly states "Finish progress bars after retro (retro stage uses the same ProgressUI)" and `finish()` is called at line 1630, after `generate_retro()`. This means the retro's LLM progress events are silently dropped, making the CLI appear to hang during the retro step.

extended_reasoning.what_the_bug_is: The progress UI is finished (progress bars cleared/stopped) before the retro generation step runs. The retro step involves an LLM call that emits workflow events consumed by the ProgressUI to show progress. Since the UI is already finished, the user sees no progress indication during what can be a multi-second LLM call.
extended_reasoning.the_specific_code_path_that_triggers_it: After the workflow engine completes (`engine.run_with_lifecycle` returns at line 1018), `progress_ui.finish()` is immediately called at line 1025. Then the retro is generated at line 1127 via `generate_retro()` which receives `Arc::clone(&emitter)`. The retro agent emits events through this emitter, which the ProgressUI would normally display — but since `finish()` was already called, the progress bars are gone.
extended_reasoning.why_existing_code_does_not_prevent_it: The comment on line 1024 even says "Finish progress bars before retro" — the incorrect ordering was intentional but wrong, contradicting the run.rs comment "Finish progress bars after retro (retro stage uses the same ProgressUI)" at line 1629.
extended_reasoning.impact: When `fabro resume` runs and retro is enabled, the CLI will appear to hang with no visible progress during the retro LLM call. This is a user experience regression compared to `fabro run`, where progress bars are shown during retro. The retro can take several seconds, leaving users confused about whether the command is stuck.
extended_reasoning.how_to_fix_it: Move the `progress_ui.lock().expect("progress lock poisoned").finish()` call from line 1025 to after the `generate_retro()` block (after line 1143), matching the ordering in run.rs.

step_by_step_proof.step: User runs `fabro resume` on a workflow with retro enabled (the default)
The workflow engine completes at line 1018
`progress_ui.finish()` is called at line 1025, clearing all progress bars
The retro generation begins at line 1127, calling `generate_retro()` with the emitter
The retro agent makes an LLM call and emits progress events through the emitter
The ProgressUI event handler receives these events but the UI is already finished — no progress is displayed
Bug: the user sees no progress indication during the retro, and the CLI appears to hang

bug.summary: resume.rs lacks a scopeguard cleanup guard for sandbox resources. In run.rs, a scopeguard ensures sandbox cleanup on panic between sandbox creation and the explicit cleanup_sandbox() call. resume.rs has no equivalent, so panics in that range will leak remote sandboxes (Daytona VMs, SSH sessions, Exe instances) indefinitely.

extended_reasoning.what_the_bug_is: The run_resumed() function in resume.rs receives an already-created sandbox (which may be a remote resource like Daytona VM, SSH session, or Exe instance) and uses it throughout the function, but never installs a scopeguard to ensure cleanup if the function panics. In contrast, run.rs installs a cleanup_guard at line 1498 that calls sandbox.cleanup() if the function unwinds due to a panic, and explicitly defuses it at line 1810 before performing the proper cleanup.
extended_reasoning.the_specific_code_path_that_triggers_it: 1. User runs `fabro resume` with --sandbox ssh, --sandbox exe, or --sandbox daytona. 2. A remote sandbox is created in prepare_from_branch() or prepare_from_checkpoint() (lines 500-581). 3. run_resumed() is called with the sandbox. 4. Any panic between line 679 (function entry) and line 1259 (cleanup_sandbox call) — for example during engine.run_with_lifecycle(), retro generation, PR creation, or any intermediate processing — causes the function to unwind without cleaning up the remote sandbox.
extended_reasoning.why_existing_code_does_not_prevent_it: The only cleanup mechanism is the explicit engine.cleanup_sandbox() call at line 1259-1261. There is no scopeguard or Drop-based safety net. The status_guard (line 712) only handles writing run status on early exit, not sandbox cleanup. The sandbox Arc itself does not implement Drop-based cleanup.
extended_reasoning.impact: Remote sandboxes (Daytona VMs, SSH sessions, Exe instances) will be leaked indefinitely if a panic occurs during resume execution. These are real cloud resources that cost money and consume capacity. While panics are uncommon in Rust, they can occur from unwrap() calls, index out-of-bounds, or poisoned mutexes — several of which exist in this code path (e.g., .expect("progress lock poisoned") at line 1025).
extended_reasoning.how_to_fix_it: Add a scopeguard cleanup guard after the sandbox is available in run_resumed(), mirroring the pattern in run.rs lines 1496-1508. Clone the sandbox Arc, create a scopeguard that spawns a cleanup task on drop, and defuse it (via ScopeGuard::into_inner) right before the explicit cleanup_sandbox() call at line 1244.

step_by_step_proof.step: User runs `fabro resume --sandbox daytona` which creates a Daytona VM sandbox in prepare_from_branch() at lines 565-579.
run_resumed() is called with the sandbox wrapped in ResumeContext at line 148.
The function proceeds through sandbox initialization, engine construction, and calls engine.run_with_lifecycle() at line 1016-1018.
A panic occurs — for example, the progress lock is poisoned at line 1025 (.expect("progress lock poisoned")), or an index-out-of-bounds in retro processing, or a poisoned mutex in the event listener.
The stack unwinds. The status_guard (line 712) was already defused at line 1022. There is no sandbox cleanup guard to catch the unwind.
The sandbox Arc is dropped without calling cleanup(). The Daytona VM (or SSH session or Exe instance) remains running indefinitely, consuming resources.
In run.rs, the equivalent scenario is handled by the cleanup_guard at line 1498, which spawns a cleanup task on panic.

bug.summary: The `prepare_from_checkpoint` function in resume.rs resolves the devcontainer config but never executes `initializeCommand` entries on the host before sandbox creation. In the old code path (`fabro run --resume`), the checkpoint resume went through the same `run_command` flow where `dc.initialize_commands` were executed on the host (lines 1056-1101 of run.rs). The new `fabro resume --checkpoint` path skips these host-side preparation commands entirely.

extended_reasoning.what_the_bug_is: When resuming from a checkpoint file with devcontainer enabled, the `prepare_from_checkpoint` function resolves the devcontainer config and extracts lifecycle phases (on_create, post_create, post_start) and environment variables, but never executes `dc.initialize_commands` on the host. In run.rs, lines 1056-1101 iterate over `dc.initialize_commands` and run each one via `tokio::process::Command::new("sh")` on the host machine before sandbox creation. This step is completely absent from resume.rs. Additionally, the `DevcontainerResolved` event is never emitted, and the daytona snapshot config override (`devcontainer_to_snapshot_config`) is never applied.
extended_reasoning.the_specific_code_path_that_triggers_it: User runs `fabro resume --checkpoint checkpoint.json --workflow my.fabro` with a project that has `sandbox.devcontainer = true` in fabro.toml and a `.devcontainer/devcontainer.json` that defines `initializeCommand` entries (e.g., creating mount directories, installing certificates, configuring host networking).
extended_reasoning.why_existing_code_does_not_prevent_it: The `prepare_from_checkpoint` function has a comment "Resolve devcontainer if enabled (mirrors run_command)" but the implementation only partially mirrors it — it extracts phases and env but omits the `initialize_commands` execution loop, the `DevcontainerResolved` event emission, and the daytona snapshot config override that exist in run.rs lines 1041-1103.
extended_reasoning.impact: For checkpoint-file resume with devcontainer enabled, host-side preparation commands will not run. This can cause sandbox creation or setup to fail if those commands are prerequisites (e.g., creating required mount directories, installing TLS certificates, setting up host networking). This is a regression from the old `fabro run --resume` behavior where these commands were always executed.
extended_reasoning.how_to_fix_it: Add the `initialize_commands` execution loop from run.rs (lines 1056-1101) into `prepare_from_checkpoint` after the devcontainer is resolved (after line 225). Also add the `DevcontainerResolved` event emission and the `devcontainer_to_snapshot_config` daytona override if applicable. The same fix should be considered for `prepare_from_branch` for consistency, though that path was also missing it in the old code.

step_by_step_proof.step: In the old code (base commit a0fbbcfe), `fabro run --resume checkpoint.json` enters `run_command()` at line 655.
The `run_command` flow resolves devcontainer at lines 1028-1111, which includes running `dc.initialize_commands` on the host at lines 1056-1101.
Only after devcontainer setup completes does the flow reach line 1544 where it loads the checkpoint and calls `engine.run_with_lifecycle()`.
In the new code, `fabro resume --checkpoint checkpoint.json` calls `prepare_from_checkpoint()` at resume.rs line 143.
`prepare_from_checkpoint` resolves devcontainer at lines 210-225 but only calls `DevcontainerResolver::resolve()` to get the config — it never iterates over `dc.initialize_commands` to execute them.
The function immediately proceeds to sandbox creation at line 245 without host-side preparation.
Bug: host-side initializeCommand entries that were executed in the old path are now silently skipped, potentially causing sandbox or workflow failures.
